messages.content: i want to know angular cli version, when i run ng -v gave me node version only

To get the Angular CLI version, you can use the following command in your terminal:

```
ng version
```

This should give you detailed information about the Angular CLI version along with other relevant information.

Node.js version v16.13.1 detected.
The Angular CLI requires a minimum Node.js version of v18.13. gives this only

It seems that the version of Node.js you have installed (v16.13.1) is not compatible with the version of Angular CLI you are trying to run. The error message indicates that the Angular CLI requires a minimum Node.js version of v18.13.

To resolve this issue, you will need to update your Node.js version to at least v18.13. You can do this by downloading and installing the latest version of Node.js from the official Node.js website: https://nodejs.org/en/

Once you have successfully updated your Node.js version, you should be able to run the Angular CLI commands without any issues.
downgrade to angular version which compitable with node v16.13.1

If you need to downgrade to an Angular CLI version that is compatible with Node.js v16.13.1, you can specify the version of the Angular CLI when you install it globally.

Here is an example of how you can install a specific version of the Angular CLI that is compatible with Node.js v16.13.1:

1. Uninstall the current version of Angular CLI:

```
npm uninstall -g @angular/cli
```

2. Install a specific version of Angular CLI:

```
npm install -g @angular/cli@{desired_version}
```

Replace `{desired_version}` with a version number that is known to be compatible with Node.js v16.13.1. You may refer to the Angular CLI release notes or documentation to find a compatible version.

After installing the desired version, you should be able to run Angular CLI commands without any compatibility issues with Node.js v16.13.1.
well
If you need further assistance or have any more questions, feel free to ask. I'm here to help!
when ng serve gives this An unhandled exception occurred: Class extends value undefined is not a constructor or null

The error message "An unhandled exception occurred: Class extends value undefined is not a constructor or null" typically indicates a compatibility issue between the version of Angular and other dependencies in your project.

To address this issue, you can try the following steps:

1. Update Angular CLI: Make sure you are using the latest version of Angular CLI by running the following command:

```
npm install -g @angular/cli
```

2. Update project dependencies: Navigate to your Angular project directory and update the project dependencies by running:

```
npm update
```

3. Clear node_modules and reinstall dependencies: Sometimes, the issue can be resolved by clearing the `node_modules` directory and reinstalling the npm dependencies. Run the following commands:

```
rm -rf node_modules
npm install
```

4. Check for incompatible dependencies: It's possible that some of your project dependencies are not compatible with each other. Review the dependencies in your `package.json` file and ensure they are compatible with the version of Angular you are using.

5. Check compatibility with Node.js: Ensure that your Node.js version is compatible with the version of Angular you are using. It's recommended to use a Node.js version that is supported by Angular.

After going through these steps, try running `ng serve` again to see if the issue has been resolved. If the problem persists, please provide more details about your project setup and dependencies so that I can assist you further.

> npm install
npm ERR! code EJSONPARSE
npm ERR! path D:\projects\oms_admin/package.json
npm ERR! JSON.parse Unexpected token "/" (0x2F) in JSON at position 255 while parsing near "...  \"dependencies\": { //bst5dmha fi code\n ..."
npm ERR! JSON.parse Failed to parse JSON data.
npm ERR! JSON.parse Note: package.json must be actual JSON, not just JavaScript.

npm ERR! A complete log of this run can be found in:

The error message indicates that there is a syntax error in your `package.json` file. The error seems to be related to a comment (`//`) in your `package.json`, which is not valid JSON syntax. Comments are not allowed in JSON files.

To resolve this issue, you need to remove the comments from your `package.json` file. Here's how you can do it:

1. Open your `package.json` file located in the `D:\projects\oms_admin` directory using a text editor.

2. Look for any lines that contain comments using `//` and remove them. Comments are not allowed in JSON files.

3. After removing the comments, save the `package.json` file.

4. Try running `npm install` again in the terminal to reinstall the project dependencies.

If you encounter any further errors or issues, please let me know the details, and I'll be happy to assist you further.

[error] TypeError: Class extends value undefined is not a constructor or null
    at Object.<anonymous> (D:\projects\oms_admin\node_modules\@angular-builders\custom-webpack\node_modules\piscina\dist\src\index.js:815:32)
    at Module._compile (node:internal/modules/cjs/loader:1101:14)
    at Object.Module._extensions..js (node:internal/modules/cjs/loader:1153:10)
    at Module.load (node:internal/modules/cjs/loader:981:32)
    at Function.Module._load (node:internal/modules/cjs/loader:822:12)
    at Module.require (node:internal/modules/cjs/loader:1005:19)
    at require (node:internal/modules/cjs/helpers:102:18)
    at Object.<anonymous> (D:\projects\oms_admin\node_modules\@angular-builders\custom-webpack\node_modules\piscina\dist\src\main.js:5:33)
    at Module._compile (node:internal/modules/cjs/loader:1101:14)
    at Object.Module._extensions..js (node:internal/modules/cjs/loader:1153:10)

The error message you are seeing indicates an issue related to the Piscina module, which is used for creating worker threads in Node.js. This error can occur due to compatibility issues or conflicts with different versions of dependencies in your project.

To resolve this issue, you can try the following steps:

1. Check Angular CLI Compatibility: Ensure that the version of Angular CLI you are using is compatible with the dependencies in your project. You can check the Angular CLI documentation or release notes for compatibility information.

2. Update Dependencies: Update the dependencies in your project to their latest compatible versions by running:

```bash
npm update
```

3. Clear Cache: Clear the npm cache and reinstall dependencies:

```bash
npm cache clean --force
rm -rf node_modules
npm install
```

4. Verify Node.js Version: Make sure you are using a Node.js version that is supported by Angular and its dependencies.

5. Temporarily Disable Custom Webpack Configuration: If you are using a custom webpack configuration, try temporarily disabling it to see if the issue is related to the webpack setup.

6. Check Other Errors: Look for any other error messages or warnings in the console output that might provide additional clues about the root cause.

After following these steps, try running your Angular project again (`ng serve`) to see if the issue has been resolved. If the problem persists, please provide more details about your project setup and any custom configurations you have applied so that I can assist you further.

npm ERR! code ERESOLVE
npm ERR! ERESOLVE unable to resolve dependency tree
npm ERR!
npm ERR! While resolving: oms-admin@0.0.0
npm ERR! Found: @angular/compiler-cli@14.3.0
npm ERR! node_modules/@angular/compiler-cli
npm ERR!   dev @angular/compiler-cli@"^14.2.0" from the root project
npm ERR!
npm ERR! Could not resolve dependency:
npm ERR! peer @angular/compiler-cli@"^17.0.0" from @angular-builders/custom-webpack@17.0.2
npm ERR! node_modules/@angular-builders/custom-webpack
npm ERR!   @angular-builders/custom-webpack@"^17.0.2" from the root project
npm ERR!
npm ERR! Fix the upstream dependency conflict, or retry
npm ERR! this command with --force, or --legacy-peer-deps
npm ERR! to accept an incorrect (and potentially broken) dependency resolution.
npm ERR!
npm ERR! See C:\Users\SaadS\AppData\Local\npm-cache\eresolve-report.txt for a full report.

The error message you are seeing indicates a dependency resolution issue with conflicting versions of `@angular/compiler-cli` in your project. This can happen when different packages require different versions of the same dependency.

To resolve this issue, you can try the following steps:

1. Update Dependencies: Update your project's dependencies to use compatible versions by running:

```bash
npm update
```

2. Clear Cache: Clear the npm cache and reinstall dependencies:

```bash
npm cache clean --force
rm -rf node_modules
npm install
```

3. Use `--force` or `--legacy-peer-deps`: You can try running `npm install` with the `--force` or `--legacy-peer-deps` flag to forcefully resolve dependencies or accept legacy peer dependencies:

```bash
npm install --force
```

or

```bash
npm install --legacy-peer-deps
```

4. Manually Fix the Dependency Conflict: You may need to manually update the conflicting dependencies in your `package.json` file to ensure that they are compatible with each other. In this case, the conflict seems to be between `@angular/compiler-cli` versions `14.3.0` and `17.0.0`.

5. Review the eresolve-report.txt: If the issue persists, you can check the `eresolve-report.txt` file located at `C:\Users\SaadS\AppData\Local\npm-cache\eresolve-report.txt` for more detailed information on the dependency resolution conflict.

After following these steps, try running `npm install` again in your project directory to see if the dependency resolution issue has been resolved. If you continue to encounter issues, please provide more information about your project setup and any custom configurations you have applied so that I can assist you further.

npm cache clean --force
npm WARN using --force Recommended protections disabled.

The warning message you received when using `npm cache clean --force` indicates that recommended protections have been disabled. This warning is displayed to make users aware that using the `--force` flag can potentially have unintended consequences.

The `npm cache clean --force` command is typically used to aggressively clean the npm cache, but it's important to be cautious when using it. If you are encountering issues related to the cache, you can try clearing the cache without the `--force` flag:

```bash
npm cache clean
```

This command will clean the npm cache without disabling the recommended protections. After cleaning the cache, you can proceed to reinstall your project dependencies by running:

```bash
rm -rf node_modules
npm install
```

This will reinstall the dependencies in your project without compromising the protections provided by npm.

If you continue to experience issues with dependency resolution or cache-related problems, please provide additional information about the specific errors or problems you are facing so that I can offer further assistance.

npm cache clean
npm ERR! As of npm@5, the npm cache self-heals from corruption issues
npm ERR!   by treating integrity mismatches as cache misses.  As a result,
npm ERR!   data extracted from the cache is guaranteed to be valid.  If you
npm ERR!   want to make sure everything is consistent, use `npm cache verify`
npm ERR!   instead.  Deleting the cache can only make npm go slower, and is
npm ERR!   not likely to correct any problems you may be encountering!
npm ERR!
npm ERR!   On the other hand, if you're debugging an issue with the installer,
npm ERR!   or race conditions that depend on the timing of writing to an empty
npm ERR!   cache, you can use `npm install --cache /tmp/empty-cache` to use a
npm ERR!   temporary cache instead of nuking the actual one.
npm ERR!
npm ERR!   If you're sure you want to delete the entire cache, rerun this command
npm ERR!   with --force.

npm ERR! A complete log of this run can be found in:
npm ERR!     C:\Users\SaadS\AppData\Local\npm-cache\_logs\2024-04-13T14_45_46_266Z-debug.log

The error message you received when trying to clean the npm cache using `npm cache clean` indicates that starting from npm version 5, the cache self-heals from corruption issues by treating integrity mismatches as cache misses. It also mentions that deleting the cache can potentially slow down npm and may not necessarily correct any problems you are encountering.

However, if you still want to delete the entire cache, you can rerun the command with the `--force` flag as suggested:

```bash
npm cache clean --force
```

Running the command with the `--force` flag will delete the entire npm cache. After cleaning the cache, you can proceed to reinstall your project dependencies:

```bash
rm -rf node_modules
npm install
```

If you continue to face issues with dependency resolution or any other problems, feel free to provide more details or error messages so that I can assist you further.

    at Module.require (node:internal/modules/cjs/loader:1005:19)
    at require (node:internal/modules/cjs/helpers:102:18)
    at Object.<anonymous> (D:\projects\oms_admin\node_modules\@angular-builders\custom-webpack\node_modules\piscina\dist\src\main.js:5:33)
    at Module._compile (node:internal/modules/cjs/loader:1101:14)
    at Object.Module._extensions..js (node:internal/modules/cjs/loader:1153:10)

The error message you are encountering seems to be related to an issue with the Piscina module, which is used for creating worker threads in Node.js. It appears that there may be a compatibility issue or conflict with different versions of dependencies in your project.

To troubleshoot and resolve this issue, you can try the following steps:

1. Update Angular CLI: Ensure that you are using the latest version of Angular CLI by running the following command:

```bash
npm install -g @angular/cli
```

2. Update Project Dependencies: Update the dependencies in your project to their latest versions to ensure compatibility:

```bash
npm update
```

3. Clear Node Modules and Reinstall Dependencies: Remove the `node_modules` directory and reinstall all dependencies:

```bash
rm -rf node_modules
npm install
```

4. Check for Custom Webpack Configurations: If you have any custom webpack configurations, review them to ensure that they are compatible with the version of Angular and other dependencies in your project.

5. Verify Node.js Version: Make sure you are using a Node.js version that is supported by Angular and its dependencies.

After following these steps, try running your Angular project again (`ng serve`) to see if the issue has been resolved. If the problem persists, please provide more details about your project setup, any custom configurations you have applied, and any specific steps that trigger the error so that I can offer further assistance.
